questions.text: The factor which can alter the equilibrium constant is
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Pressure changes can shift the position of equilibrium for reactions involving gases, but they do not change the equilibrium constant itself.
Temperature is the only factor that can change the equilibrium constant. It affects the kinetic energy of molecules, influencing reaction rates and equilibrium.
A catalyst speeds up both the forward and reverse reactions equally without altering the equilibrium constant.
Changing the concentration of reactants or products shifts the equilibrium position but does not change the equilibrium constant.
This option is incorrect because temperature does indeed alter the equilibrium constant.

questions.explanation: The equilibrium constant is a measure of the ratio of concentrations of products to reactants at equilibrium for a given temperature. It is only affected by changes in temperature, not by changes in pressure, concentration, or the presence of a catalyst. Pressure and concentration changes can shift the equilibrium position but do not alter the constant value. Catalysts speed up the attainment of equilibrium without changing the equilibrium constant.

questions.text: The reaction will proceed in the reverse direction when:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: When Qc is less than Kc, the reaction proceeds in the forward direction to reach equilibrium, not the reverse direction.
When Qc is greater than Kc, the reaction will proceed in the reverse direction to re-establish equilibrium, reducing the concentration of products.
If Qc equals Kc, the system is already at equilibrium, and no net reaction occurs in either direction.
This option is incorrect because it does not relate logically or mathematically to the condition for a reaction to proceed in the reverse direction.

questions.explanation: If Qc < Kc, the reaction moves forward (to make more products).If Qc > Kc, the reaction moves backward (to make more reactants).If Qc = Kc, the reaction is at equilibrium (no net change).

questions.text: For which reaction, the volume is an irrelevant factor in determining Ke
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: The formation of an ester typically involves a liquid-phase reaction, which means volume changes do not significantly impact the equilibrium constant. However, this does not make volume entirely irrelevant in all conditions.
The dissociation of PCl5 involves a change in the number of moles of gases, as PCl5 dissociates into PCl3 and Cl2. This change affects the equilibrium constant with volume changes.
In the decomposition of N2O4 into 2NO2, the number of moles of gas remains the same on both sides of the equation, making volume a non-factor in determining the equilibrium constant.
The synthesis of NH3 involves a change in the number of moles of gases, as N2 and H2 combine to form NH3, making volume relevant in determining the equilibrium constant.

questions.explanation: This is typically a liquid-phase reaction involving liquids (like acid + alcohol → ester + water).Volume changes do not significantly affect equilibrium in liquid phase because concentrations are based on molarity, and liquids are nearly incompressible.So, volume is irrelevant for equilibrium

questions.text: For a reaction proceeding with increase in no. of moles, which of these can affect in more than one ways.
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: An increase in volume shifts the equilibrium to the side with more moles of gas, which is a single directional effect.
An increase in pressure shifts the equilibrium to the side with fewer moles of gas, which is also a single directional effect.

Changes equilibrium constant K by affecting reaction enthalpy (ΔH).Shifts equilibrium depending on whether reaction is exo- or endothermic.Also affects reaction rates because temperature changes kinetic energy.Affects in more than one way:
Speeds up both forward and backward reaction rates equally.Does not affect equilibrium position or KAffects only reaction rate, not equilibrium position.Affects in more than one way: no.

questions.explanation: Increase in volume — YES (affects equilibrium position and concentration/rate)Increase in pressure — YES (affects equilibrium position and rate)Change in temperature — YES (affects equilibrium constant and rate)Catalyst — NO (only affects rate, not equilibrium)

questions.text: What will be the effect of addition of catalyst at room temperature?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. A catalyst does not affect the equilibrium constant; it only speeds up the attainment of equilibrium by providing an alternative pathway with lower activation energy.
This option is correct. A catalyst lowers the activation energy of a reaction, allowing the reaction to proceed more quickly without altering the equilibrium position.

options.text: Kf and Kb will increase to the same extent
options.explanation: This option is incorrect. While a catalyst speeds up the forward (Kf) and backward (Kb) reactions equally, it does not change their individual values or the equilibrium constant.
This option is incorrect. A catalyst does not alter the equilibrium composition of a reaction; it only helps the system reach equilibrium faster.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is that the activation energy will decrease. A catalyst provides an alternative reaction pathway with a lower activation energy, thus increasing the rate of both the forward and reverse reactions. However, it does not change the equilibrium constant or the equilibrium composition of the reaction. The equilibrium constant is determined by the relative energies of the reactants and products, which remain unchanged by a catalyst. Therefore, options stating changes to the equilibrium constant or composition are incorrect.

questions.text: The equilibrium, solid ⇌ liquid ⇌ gas will shift in the forward direction when
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Increasing temperature favors the endothermic reaction by shifting the equilibrium towards the right, resulting in more liquid and gas formation from the solid. This is the correct choice.
When temperature remains constant, there is no external influence to shift the equilibrium towards any direction. Therefore, the system remains unchanged, and this option is incorrect.
Lowering the temperature favors the exothermic reaction, shifting the equilibrium towards the left. Thus, less liquid and gas form, making this option incorrect.
Changing pressure affects gaseous equilibria but has minimal effect on this particular solid-liquid-gas equilibrium, especially when moles of gas do not change. Thus, this choice is incorrect.

questions.explanation: The equilibrium involving solid, liquid, and gas phases described here is an endothermic process. Raising the temperature provides the energy needed for the solid to transition into liquid and subsequently into gas, shifting the equilibrium to the right. This supports the forward direction of the reaction. In contrast, keeping the temperature constant or lowering it does not provide the energy required for the endothermic process, hence the equilibrium remains unchanged or shifts left. Pressure changes have no significant effect on this solid-liquid-gas equilibrium, as they primarily impact equilibria involving gases with differing moles.

questions.text: The exothermic formation of ClF3 is represented by the equation:Cl2 (g) + 3F2 (g) ⇋ 2ClF3 (g) ΔH=-329kJWhich of the following will increase the quantity of ClF3 in an equilibrium mixture of F2,Cl2 and ClF3?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Adding more F2 increases the concentration of one of the reactants, which, according to Le Chatelier's Principle, will shift the equilibrium towards the products to counteract the change, increasing the production of ClF3. Therefore, this option is correct.

options.text: Increasing the volume of the container
options.explanation: Increasing the volume of the container decreases the pressure of the system. Since there are more moles of gas on the reactant side (1 mole of Cl2 and 3 moles of F2) compared to the product side (2 moles of ClF3), the equilibrium will shift towards the reactants, decreasing ClF3 production. Therefore, this option is incorrect.

Removing Cl2 reduces the concentration of one of the reactants, causing the equilibrium to shift towards the reactants to replace the removed Cl2. This shift decreases the production of ClF3, making this option incorrect.

Increasing the temperature adds energy to the system. Since the reaction is exothermic (releases heat), the equilibrium will shift towards the reactants to absorb the additional heat, decreasing the production of ClF3. Thus, this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is to add F2. According to Le Chatelier's Principle, adding more of a reactant shifts the equilibrium towards the products to counteract the change, increasing the concentration of ClF3. Increasing the volume of the container decreases the pressure, which shifts the equilibrium towards the reactants (since there are more moles of gas on the reactant side), thus reducing ClF3 production. Removing Cl2 decreases its concentration, causing the equilibrium to shift towards the reactants to replace the missing Cl2, also reducing ClF3 production. Increasing the temperature shifts the equilibrium towards the reactants because the reaction is exothermic, thus also reducing ClF3 production.

questions.text: Which of the following equilibrium does not get affected with the change in volume of the system?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This equilibrium is affected by volume changes because Δn ≠ 0. The number of moles changes from 1 to 2, which means the equilibrium will shift with volume changes.
Here, Δn ≠ 0 because the total moles of gases decrease from 4 to 2. A change in volume will affect this equilibrium.
This reaction has Δn ≠ 0, with moles increasing from 1 to 2, indicating that a change in volume will shift the equilibrium position.
In this case, Δn = 0 as the moles of reactants and products are equal (2 moles on each side). Therefore, the equilibrium is unaffected by changes in volume, making this option correct.

questions.explanation: The correct option is Option D because the change in volume does not affect the equilibrium when Δn = 0, meaning the number of moles of gases on the reactant side equals the number of moles on the product side. In Option D, both sides have 2 moles of gases, so volume changes will not shift the equilibrium. In contrast, Options A, B, and C have different numbers of moles on each side, meaning their equilibria will shift with volume changes.

questions.text: For a reaction if Kp>Kc , the forward reaction is favoured by
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. Temperature affects the equilibrium constant itself but is not directly related to the comparison between Kp and Kc.
This option is incorrect. Temperature changes can affect the equilibrium position but do not explain the relationship where Kp > Kc.

This option is correct. When Kp > Kc, it indicates that the reaction involves an increase in the number of moles of gas. Lowering the pressure will favor the side with more moles of gas, which is typically the product side in this scenario.
This option is incorrect. High pressure favors the side of the reaction with fewer moles of gas, which is contrary to the requirement when Kp > Kc.

questions.explanation: According to the relation Kp = Kc(RT)Δn, if Kp > Kc, then Δn > 0, meaning the number of moles of gas increases on the product side of the reaction. To favor the forward reaction under these conditions, one would decrease the pressure. Decreasing pressure shifts the equilibrium towards the side with more moles of gas—in this case, the product side. Temperature changes are not directly related to this comparison, hence both high and low temperature options are incorrect. High pressure, which favors the side with fewer moles, is also incorrect in this context.

questions.text: In the Haber process for making ammonia, an increase in pressure favors
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: The forward reaction is favored because increasing pressure shifts the equilibrium towards the side with fewer gas molecules. In the Haber process, N2 + 3H2 ↔ 2NH3, there are four molecules on the reactant side and two on the product side, so the production of ammonia is favored.
The reverse reaction is not favored because it would increase the number of gas molecules, which opposes the effect of increased pressure.
One of the reactions must be favored due to the change in pressure, so this option is incorrect.
Both reactions cannot be favored simultaneously under increased pressure, as the shift must favor the side with fewer gas molecules.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is that an increase in pressure favors the forward reaction in the Haber process. This is because the forward reaction results in fewer gas molecules: N2 + 3H2 ↔ 2NH3. With an increase in pressure, the system will adjust to minimize this change by favoring the production of fewer gas molecules, hence the forward reaction is favored. Option B is incorrect as the reverse reaction would increase the number of gas molecules, opposing the pressure increase. Option C is incorrect because the pressure change does affect equilibrium, and therefore one reaction is favored. Option D is incorrect since both reactions cannot be favored at the same time with respect to pressure changes.

questions.text: In one second if the concentration changes from 0.1 to 0.25 then the rate will be:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: In one second if the concentration changes from 0.1 to 0.25 then the rate will not be 0.02 moles/dm3s-1.
In one second if the concentration changes from 0.1 to 0.25 then the rate will not be 0.03 moles/dm3s-1.
Rate of reaction = change in concentration/time = 0.15mol dm-3/1s = 0.15 mol dm-3 s-1
In one second if the concentration changes from 0.1 to 0.25 then the rate will not be 0.11 moles/dm3s-1.

questions.explanation: Rate of reaction = change in concentration/time = 0.15mol dm-3/1s = 0.15 mol dm-3 s-1

questions.text: The decomposition of ozone has a very high value of the equilibrium constant because of its?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Stability is a measure of how resistant a molecule is to decomposition. A stable molecule is one that is not easily decomposed. The decomposition of ozone is a very exothermic reaction, meaning that it releases a lot of energy. This energy can break the bonds between the oxygen atoms in ozone, making it more likely to decompose.

Reactivity: Reactivity is a measure of how likely a molecule is to react with other molecules. A reactive molecule is one that is easily activated and reacts with other molecules quickly. The decomposition of ozone is a very rapid reaction, so ozone is a very reactive molecule.

Compressibility: Compressibility is a measure of how much a substance can be compressed. A compressible substance is one that can be squeezed into a smaller volume. The decomposition of ozone does not affect its compressibility.

The decomposition of ozone has a very high value of the equilibrium constant because of its instability. Ozone is a molecule that is made up of three oxygen atoms. The bonds between the oxygen atoms in ozone are very weak, so ozone is easily decomposed into oxygen gas. The equilibrium constant is a measure of how much of a product is formed when a reactant is decomposed. A high value of the equilibrium constant means that a lot of product is formed. In the case of ozone decomposition, the high value of the equilibrium constant means that a lot of oxygen gas is formed when ozone is decomposed. The instability of ozone is due to the fact that the three oxygen atoms in ozone are arranged in a triangular shape. This shape puts a lot of strain on the bonds between the oxygen atoms, making them more likely to break. The instability of ozone is a problem because ozone is a very important molecule in the atmosphere. Ozone absorbs ultraviolet radiation from the sun, preventing it from reaching the Earth's surface. The depletion of ozone in the atmosphere has been linked to an increase in skin cancer and other health problems.

questions.explanation: The decomposition of ozone has a very high value of the equilibrium constant because of its instability. Ozone is a molecule that is made up of three oxygen atoms. The bonds between the oxygen atoms in ozone are very weak, so ozone is easily decomposed into oxygen gas. The equilibrium constant is a measure of how much of a product is formed when a reactant is decomposed. A high value of the equilibrium constant means that a lot of product is formed. In the case of ozone decomposition, the high value of the equilibrium constant means that a lot of oxygen gas is formed when ozone is decomposed. The instability of ozone is due to the fact that the three oxygen atoms in ozone are arranged in a triangular shape. This shape puts a lot of strain on the bonds between the oxygen atoms, making them more likely to break. The instability of ozone is a problem because ozone is a very important molecule in the atmosphere. Ozone absorbs ultraviolet radiation from the sun, preventing it from reaching the Earth's surface. The depletion of ozone in the atmosphere has been linked to an increase in skin cancer and other health problems.

questions.text: The catalyst in the formation of ester from alcohol and a weak acid is?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: a) A base:Option (a) "A base" is not the correct answer. A base is not used as a catalyst in the formation of an ester from alcohol and a weak acid. In esterification, the catalyst is typically an acid or an acid derivative.
b) Palladium:Option (b) "Palladium" is not the correct answer. Palladium is a metal and is not commonly used as a catalyst in the esterification process.
c) Nickel:Option (c) "Nickel" is not the correct answer either. Like palladium, nickel is a metal and is not typically used as a catalyst in the formation of esters from alcohols and weak acids.

d) A mineral acid:Option (d) "A mineral acid" is the correct answer. In the formation of an ester from alcohol and a weak acid, a mineral acid is often used as a catalyst. Mineral acids are strong inorganic acids such as sulfuric acid (H2SO4) or hydrochloric acid (HCl). These acids can protonate the alcohol, making it more susceptible to nucleophilic attack by the weak acid, leading to the formation of an ester.

questions.explanation: Esterification is the process of combining an organic acid such as carboxylic acid with an alcohol to form ester. The catalyst used is a concentrated sulphuric acid which is a strong mineral acid Options A , B & C are incorrect.

questions.text: Choose the one which is not an assumption of the collision theory of reaction rate:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: For chemical reaction to occur molecules/particles must collide
options.explanation: Options A is the assumption of collision theory of reaction rates.

options.text: For reacting molecules/particles must possess a certain minimum amount of energy which is the activation energy
options.explanation: For reacting molecules/particles must possess a certain minimum amount of energy which is the activation energy is the assumptions of collision theory of reaction rates.

options.text: For hydrogen molecule formation, atoms require specific orientation
options.explanation: Collision theory states that the rate of a chemical reaction is proportional to the number of collisions between reactant molecules. The more often reactant molecules collide, the more often they react with one another, and the faster the reaction rate. Hydrogen don’t require a specific orientation because it’s a diatomic molecule.
Every collision is not productive is the assumption of collision theory of reaction rates.

questions.explanation: Collision molecular theory says that for a reaction to occur, reactants must collide in a specific orientation with a specific energy. As hydrogen is a diatomic molecule so it does not require a specific orientation.

questions.text: The conversion of Nitrogen to ammonia or nitrogenous compounds is called
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Nitrification is a microbial process by which ammonia is oxidised to nitrite or nitrate.

Nitrogen fixation is when molecular nitrogen is converted into ammonia or other nitrogenous compounds. It is part of the nitrogen cycle and is carried out naturally by certain bacteria in soil to provide plants with vital nutrients.It can also be carried out artificially in industries. Nitrification is a microbial process by which ammonia is oxidised to nitrite or nitrate. Denitrification is the microbial process of reducing nitrates and nitrite into gaseous forms of nitrogen.
Denitrification is the microbial process of reducing nitrates and nitrite into gaseous forms of nitrogen.

Nitrogen assimilation is the formation of organic nitrogen compounds like amino acids from inorganic nitrogen compounds present in the environment. Organisms like plants, fungi and certain bacteria that can fix nitrogen gas (N2) depend on the ability to assimilate nitrate or ammonia for their needs.

questions.explanation: Nitrogen fixation is when molecular nitrogen is converted into ammonia or other nitrogenous compounds. It is part of the nitrogen cycle and is carried out naturally by certain bacteria in soil to provide plants with vital nutrients.It can also be carried out artificially in industries. Nitrification is a microbial process by which ammonia is oxidised to nitrite or nitrate. Denitrification is the microbial process of reducing nitrates and nitrite into gaseous forms of nitrogen. Nitrogen assimilation is the formation of organic nitrogen compounds like amino acids from inorganic nitrogen compounds present in the environment. Organisms like plants, fungi and certain bacteria that can fix nitrogen gas (N2) depend on the ability to assimilate nitrate or ammonia for their needs.

questions.text: Concentration of reactants and products are expressed as moles per unit _.
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Reaction rates are usually expressed as the concentration of reactant consumed or the concentration of product formed per unit time. The units are thus moles per liter per unit time, written as M/s, M/min, or M/h.

Reaction rates are usually expressed as the concentration of reactant consumed or the concentration of product formed per unit time. The units are thus moles per liter per unit time, written as M/s, M/min, or M/h.

The amount of reactant and product or the number of moles of reactant or product present in a container is known as the concentration. When the reactant or products are present within a three dimensional container, concentration becomes moles per unit volume. Thus, option C is correct.

The amount of reactant and product or the number of moles of reactant or product present in a container is known as the concentration. When the reactant or products are present within a three dimensional container, concentration becomes moles per unit volume.

questions.explanation: The amount of reactant and product or the number of moles of reactant or product present in a container is known as the concentration. When the reactant or products are present within a three dimensional container, concentration becomes moles per unit volume. Thus, option C is correct.

questions.text: The coefficients of balanced equation are a part of equilibrium constant value as _.
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Denominator includes reactants of balanced chemical equation in kc constant value: A+B—-->C+D Kc= [C] [D]/[A] [B]
Numerator includes products of balanced chemical equation A+B—-->C+D Kc= [C] [D] /[A] [B] Where C and D are products.
coefficient are written as the power of concentration in equilibrium constant value aA +bB-->cC +dD Kc= [C]^c [D]^d / [A]^a [B)^b
Only C is the suitable answer

questions.explanation: Reason: In Equilibrium equations, the concentration of products and reactants is raised to their respective coefficients. This can be seen in the equation: Kc=[C]c[D]d/[A]a{B]b.

questions.text: With different number of moles of reactants and product, the volume of system:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Option A is incorrect since the volume of the system does not remain unchanged with a change in the number of moles.

B is correct since Avogadro's law states that under the same conditions of temperature in pressure equal volumes of different gasses contain an equal number of molecules so the volume of a gaseous system is related to the number of moles and when the number of mole changes the volume of the system changes.
Option C is incorrect since the volume of a system does not always decrease with different numbers of moles.
Option D is incorrect since the volume of a system does not always increase with different numbers of moles.

questions.explanation: Option B is correct since Avogadro’s law states that under the same conditions of temperature and pressure, equal volumes of different gases contain an equal number of molecules. So the volume of a gaseous system is related to the number of moles, and when the number of moles changes, the volume of the system changes. Option A is incorrect since the volume of the system does not remain unchanged with a change in the number of moles. Option C is incorrect since the volume of a system does not always decrease with different numbers of moles. Option D is incorrect since the volume of a system does not always increase with different numbers of moles.

questions.text: Rate of reaction increases with increase by increasing temperature because:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: The concentration of the reaction increases
options.explanation: The rate of a reaction does not increase due to an increase in temperature because of an increase in the concentration of reactants. While an increase in concentration can increase the rate of a reaction, this is not the reason why the rate of a reaction increases with an increase in temperature. As stated earlier, the rate of a reaction increases with an increase in temperature because of the increase in kinetic energy of the reactant molecules, leading to more effective collisions and a higher reaction rate.

options.text: The activation energy of the reaction increases
options.explanation: The rate of a reaction does not increase with an increase in temperature because the activation energy increases. The opposite is true. An increase in temperature leads to an increase in the kinetic energy of the reactant molecules, which leads to more effective collisions and a higher reaction rate. This increase in kinetic energy also helps more molecules overcome the activation energy barrier, which is why the rate of a reaction increases with an increase in temperature.

The rate of a reaction increases with an increase in temperature because of the increase in kinetic energy of the reactant molecules. As the temperature increases, the kinetic energy of the reactant molecules increases, and more molecules have sufficient energy to overcome the activation energy barrier, leading to more effective collisions and a higher reaction rate.

This option is incorrect. The rate of a reaction increases with an increase in temperature because of the increase in kinetic energy of the reactant molecules. As the temperature increases, the kinetic energy of the reactant molecules increases, and more molecules have sufficient energy to overcome the activation energy barrier, leading to more effective collisions and a higher reaction rate.

questions.explanation: The rate of a reaction increases with an increase in temperature because of the increase in kinetic energy of the reactant molecules. As the temperature increases, the kinetic energy of the reactant molecules increases, and more molecules have sufficient energy to overcome the activation energy barrier, leading to more effective collisions and a higher reaction rate.

questions.text: At equilibrium the reaction mixture contains:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This option states that at equilibrium, the reaction mixture contains only the products and no reactants. This is not correct. At equilibrium, both reactants and products are present in the reaction mixture, but the concentrations remain constant as the forward and reverse reactions are occurring at the same rate.

This option states that at equilibrium, the reaction mixture contains only the reactants and no products. This is also not correct. At equilibrium, both reactants and products are present, but their concentrations remain constant.

At equilibrium, a reaction mixture contains both reactants and products. An equilibrium is a state where the forward and reverse reactions in a chemical reaction occur at the same rate, so the concentrations of the reactants and products no longer change with time. At equilibrium, the concentrations of the reactants and products are in a dynamic balance, where the rates of the forward and reverse reactions are equal.

This option states that at equilibrium, the reaction mixture contains neither reactants nor products. This is not correct. As mentioned earlier, both reactants and products are present at equilibrium.

questions.explanation: Option C is correct since, at equilibrium, the forward and reverse reactions occur at equal rates, and the concentrations of products and reactants remain constant. Hence both are present in the reaction mixture.

questions.text: The rate of reaction:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This is incorrect. As the reaction proceeds, reactant concentrations decrease, which typically causes the rate of reaction to decrease, not increase.
This is correct. The rate of reaction generally decreases as the reaction proceeds because the reactants are being used up, leading to lower concentrations, which in turn reduces the reaction rate.
This is incorrect. The rate of reaction usually changes as the concentrations of reactants change. As they decrease, the rate tends to decrease.
This is incorrect because one of the provided options accurately describes the change in the rate of reaction as it proceeds.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is that the rate of reaction decreases as the reaction proceeds. This is due to the reduction in reactant concentrations as they are converted into products. Since reaction rate is dependent on the concentration of reactants, a decrease in these concentrations leads to a decrease in the rate. Option A is incorrect because it suggests the rate increases, which contradicts the typical behavior of reactions. Option C is incorrect because it suggests the rate remains unchanged, which is not the case as the reaction typically slows down. Option D is incorrect as Option B provides the correct description.

questions.text: For the equilibrium reaction 2NO2 ⇌ N2O4 (g) (-61 kJ), increase of temperature would:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Option A is wrong according to given reaction conditions.

If an exothermic reaction in equilibrium experiences a rise in temperature, the reaction shifts to the left toward reactants. An increase in temperature will favour the reverse reaction. So, N2O4 will undergo decomposition.
Option C is wrong according to given reaction conditions.
Option D is wrong according to given reaction conditions.

questions.explanation: The reaction is exothermic (ΔH = -61 kJ) in the forward direction (2NO₂ → N₂O₄). Increasing temperature adds heat, which the system counteracts by shifting toward the endothermic reverse reaction. Thus, the equilibrium shifts to decompose N₂O₄ into NO₂.

questions.text: In the reaction, 2SO2 (g) + O2 ⇌ 2SO3 (g) ∆H= -X cal most favorable conditions of temperature and pressure for a greater yield of SO3 are:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: While low temperature favors the exothermic reaction, low pressure does not favor the formation of the product as the reaction involves a decrease in the number of moles of gas.

High temperature is not favorable for exothermic reactions, and low pressure does not favor a shift towards the side with fewer gas moles. Therefore, this combination is not ideal for maximizing SO3 yield.
High temperature is unfavorable for exothermic reactions, but high pressure favors the formation of the product by shifting the equilibrium to the side with fewer moles of gas.
Low temperature favors the exothermic reaction, and high pressure shifts the equilibrium towards the side with fewer moles of gas, both of which enhance the yield of SO3.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option D: Low temperature and high pressure. According to Le Chatelier's principle, increasing the pressure will shift the equilibrium towards the side with fewer gas moles, which is the product side (2SO3), resulting in a higher yield of SO3. Additionally, since the reaction is exothermic (∆H = -X cal), lowering the temperature will also favor the forward reaction, further increasing the yield of SO3. The combination of low temperature and high pressure is thus the most favorable for maximizing the production of SO3. Other options either incorrectly prioritize temperature and pressure conditions that do not favor the formation of SO3 or apply an incorrect understanding of Le Chatelier's principle.

questions.text: A system at equilibrium can be disturbed by:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: A system at equilibrium can be disturbed by changes in temperature, pressure, or the concentrations of reactants or products. This disturbance can cause the system to shift in the direction that opposes the change, ultimately re-establishing a new equilibrium. Therefore option D is correct.

A system at equilibrium can be disturbed by changes in temperature, pressure, or the concentrations of reactants or products. This disturbance can cause the system to shift in the direction that opposes the change, ultimately re-establishing a new equilibrium. Therefore option D is correct.

A system at equilibrium can be disturbed by changes in temperature, pressure, or the concentrations of reactants or products. This disturbance can cause the system to shift in the direction that opposes the change, ultimately re-establishing a new equilibrium. Therefore option D is correct.

Systems at equilibrium can be disturbed by changes to temperature, concentration, and, in some cases, volume and pressure; volume and pressure changes will disturb equilibrium if the number of moles of gas is different on the reactant and product sides of the reaction. The effects of these variables on the equilibria can be summarised in the table below:

questions.explanation: Systems at equilibrium can be disturbed by changes to temperature, concentration, and, in some cases, volume and pressure; volume and pressure changes will disturb equilibrium if the number of moles of gas is different on the reactant and product sides of the reaction.

questions.text: One mole of glucose was dissolved in 1 kg of water, ethanol, ether, and benzene separately and the molal boiling point constant of each solution was found to be 0.52, 1.75,2.16, and 2.70 in the units of / °C kg mol-1 respectively. questions.text: When a reaction proceeds, what will happen to the concentration of reactants and products with time?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: As option C is correct so this option is not possible.

options.text: Concentration of reactants increases and of products decreases
options.explanation: As a reaction proceeds towards equilibrium, the concentrations of reactants generally decrease, while the concentrations of products increase. Initially, at the start of the reaction, the concentration changes are more pronounced. However, as the system approaches equilibrium, these changes become smaller until the concentrations stabilize. At equilibrium, the concentrations of both reactants and products remain constant over time. It's important to note that not all reactions go to completion; some reach an equilibrium state where the forward and reverse reactions occur at the same rate. In such cases, the concentrations of reactants and products no longer change, indicating a dynamic balance between the two processes. Therefore this option is not correct.

options.text: Concentration of products increases and reactants decreases
options.explanation: As a reaction proceeds towards equilibrium, the concentrations of reactants generally decrease, while the concentrations of products increase. Initially, at the start of the reaction, the concentration changes are more pronounced. However, as the system approaches equilibrium, these changes become smaller until the concentrations stabilize. At equilibrium, the concentrations of both reactants and products remain constant over time. It's important to note that not all reactions go to completion; some reach an equilibrium state where the forward and reverse reactions occur at the same rate. In such cases, the concentrations of reactants and products no longer change, indicating a dynamic balance between the two processes. Therefore this option is correct.

options.text: Concentration of both reactants and products increases

In such cases, the concentrations of reactants and products no longer change, indicating a dynamic balance between the two processes. Therefore this option is not correct.

questions.text: A chemist who is concerned with large-scale manufacture of useful compounds is primarily interested in
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Incorrect option. Minimizing energy consumption is not always the primary concern for a chemist who is focused on large-scale manufacturing. In some cases, it may be necessary to use more energy in order to obtain a higher yield of the desired product.

Correct option. The chemist is primarily interested in minimizing the reverse reaction. This is because a reverse reaction will reduce the yield of the desired product, which means that the chemist will need to use more reactants to produce the same amount of product. This can be costly and time-consuming, especially for large-scale manufacturing.

Incorrect option. Maximizing the backward reaction is typically not the goal of a chemist who is manufacturing useful compounds. A backward reaction is a reaction that proceeds in the opposite direction of the desired reaction, and it can reduce the yield of the desired product.

options.text: Decreasing the acidity of the product

questions.explanation: In large-scale manufacturing (like Haber’s process or Contact process), the chemist wants to push the equilibrium toward products.This means making conditions that slow down or suppress the backward reaction, so yield remains high.This is the main chemical goal.

questions.text: The reaction PCI5(g) ⇌ PCI3(s) + Cl2(g) is an example of
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: A backward reaction is the reverse of the forward reaction. It is a chemical reaction that proceeds from products to reactants. In a backward reaction, products are consumed to form reactants. The backward reaction can occur in the opposite direction of the forward reaction when the conditions are changed.

An irreversible reaction is a chemical reaction that proceeds in only one direction and cannot be reversed under normal conditions. In an irreversible reaction, the reactants are consumed to form products, and once the products are formed, they cannot be converted back into the reactants. The reaction proceeds to completion, and the products cannot react to form the reactants.

A forward reaction is a chemical reaction that proceeds from reactants to products. It is the reaction that occurs in the direction written in the chemical equation. In a forward reaction, reactants are consumed to form products.

A reversible reaction is a reaction that can proceed in both the forward and reverse directions. In the case of the reaction PCI5(g) ⇌ PCI3 (s) + Cl2 (g), the reaction can proceed in both the forward and reverse directions. In the forward direction, PCI5(g) reacts to form PCI3(s) and Cl2(g). In the reverse direction, PCI3(s) and Cl2(g) react to form PCI5(g). The reaction can reach a state of equilibrium where the rate of the forward reaction is equal to the rate of the reverse reaction, and the concentrations of the reactants and products remain constant over time. The equilibrium constant (K) for the reaction can be calculated using the concentrations of the reactants and products at equilibrium, and it describes the extent to which the reaction proceeds in the forward or reverse direction under specific conditions.

questions.explanation: Reversible vs irreversibleIrreversible means it only goes in one direction until completion — not the case here.Reversible means it can reach equilibrium with both forward and backward reactions happening simultaneously — this matches the given equation.Forward or backward reactionForward reaction: PCl₅ → PCl₃ + Cl₂Backward reaction: PCl₃ + Cl₂ → PCl₅The equation shows both directions, so it’s not just forward or just backward.

options.explanation: Rate of forward reaction = Rate of backward reaction.Concentrations remain constant (but not necessarily equal).This is the true condition for chemical equilibrium.

options.text: Both backward and forward reactions stop
options.explanation: That would be a static situation, not chemical equilibrium.In equilibrium, reactions are ongoing at the molecular level

options.text: Concentration of each of the reactions and products becomes equal
options.explanation: Concentrations at equilibrium are constant, but not necessarily equal.For example: In Haber process, equilibrium mixture has far more reactants than products, yet it’s still in equilibrium.

questions.explanation: Equilibrium ≠ reactions stopEquilibrium = reactions run at equal speeds in opposite directions

questions.text: The equilibrium between water and its vapor, in an open vessel
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: In an open vessel, any vapor formed escapes into the surroundings.Since vapor keeps leaving, the reverse process (condensation back to liquid) is negligible → no dynamic equilibrium forms.

Reason: For equilibrium between water and vapor, the rate of evaporation must equal the rate of condensation.In an open container, vapor escapes into the atmosphere, so condensation rate is nearly zero → equilibrium is impossible.
In an open vessel, the vapor is not confined, so pressure changes won't stop vapor escape — equilibrium still won't be achieved.
Temperature affects the evaporation rate, but in an open vessel, vapor still escapes, so equilibrium is never established at any temperature.

questions.explanation: Closed vessel → equilibrium possible (dynamic balance between evaporation & condensation)Open vessel → no equilibrium (vapor lost to surroundings)

questions.text: The rate of formation of ammonia is not economical at?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: While the rate of ammonia formation is slow at low temperatures, it is true that low temperatures favor the formation of ammonia according to Le Chatelier's principle. However, the yield of ammonia is low at low temperatures. Therefore, the optimal temperature for ammonia synthesis is typically around 450-550°C, which provides a balance between the rate of ammonia formation and the yield of ammonia.

The rate of formation of ammonia is higher at higher temperatures, but the yield of ammonia decreases at higher temperatures. This is because the forward reaction (the formation of ammonia from nitrogen and hydrogen) is exothermic, meaning it releases heat. According to Le Chatelier's principle, increasing the temperature of the reaction will shift the equilibrium towards the side of the reaction that absorbs heat, which in this case is the reactant's side. Therefore, increasing the temperature of the reaction will increase the rate of ammonia formation, but it will also decrease the yield of ammonia. As a result, the optimal temperature for ammonia synthesis is typically around 450-550°C, which provides a balance between the rate of ammonia formation and the yield of ammonia.

While the rate of ammonia formation is slow at low temperatures, low temperatures indeed favor the formation of ammonia according to Le Chatelier's principle. However, the yield of ammonia is low at low temperatures. At high temperatures, the reaction rate increases due to higher kinetic energy and more frequent collisions.However, because the reaction is exothermic, high temperatures shift the equilibrium position backward (towards nitrogen and hydrogen), reducing the yield of ammonia. optimum temp is used

While the rate of ammonia formation is slow at low temperatures, low temperatures indeed favor the formation of ammonia according to Le Chatelier's principle. However, the yield of ammonia is low at low temperatures.

questions.explanation: While the rate of ammonia formation is slow at low temperatures, low temperatures indeed favor the formation of ammonia according to Le Chatelier's principle. However, the yield of ammonia is low at low temperatures. Therefore, the optimal temperature for ammonia synthesis is typically around 450-550°C, which provides a balance between the rate of ammonia formation and the yield of ammonia.

questions.text: What will happen to the KClO3 when KCl is added to its solution in water?

options.explanation: Option A is incorrect because adding KCl introduces a common ion (Cl⁻), which suppresses KClO₃ solubility via the common‑ion effect.

When KCl is added to a solution of KClO₃, it introduces a common ion (K⁺) into the solution. Due to the common ion effect, the dissociation of KClO₃ is suppressed. As a result, the solubility of KClO₃ decreases, so the correct answer is B.
Option C is incorrect because adding KCl increases the total Cl⁻ concentration, not decreases it in the solution overall.
Option D is incorrect because KCl addition does not affect KClO₃ dissociation, so ClO₃⁻ concentration remains unchanged essentially.

questions.explanation: The law of mass action states that the rate of a chemical reaction is proportional to the product of the concentrations of the reactants. It was formulated by the Norwegian chemists C.M. Guldberg and P. Waage in the 19th century.

questions.text: A species which is formed by the acceptance of proton by a base is called _.
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: An acid is a chemical species that donates or releases protons (H+) when dissolved in water. Acids are characterized by their ability to increase the concentration of hydrogen ions in a solution. They are often sour-tasting and can react with bases to form salts.

Conjugate acid is formed when a base accepts a proton (H+). It is the species that is created by the addition of a proton to a base during a chemical reaction. In an acid-base reaction, the base accepts a proton to become its conjugate acid.

Conjugate base is formed when an acid donates a proton (H+). It is the species that remains after the acid loses a proton during a chemical reaction. In an acid-base reaction, the acid loses a proton to become its conjugate base

A salt is a chemical compound composed of positive and negative ions that are held together by ionic bonds. Salts are formed through the reaction between an acid and a base, where the acid donates a proton and the base accepts it. Salts are often formed as a result of neutralizing reactions between acids and bases.

questions.explanation: In the question, you are asked to identify the species formed by the acceptance of a proton by a base, which is called a conjugate acid. It is the species that is created by the addition of a proton to a base during a chemical reaction. In an acid-base reaction, the base accepts a proton to become its conjugate acid. Hence, Option B is the correct answer.
hints: formed by the acceptance of proton

Option 2 is incorrect. The graph at this point shows varying concentrations, which means the system is adjusting and has not reached equilibrium.
Option 3 is incorrect. While the rate of change may be slowing, the concentrations are not yet constant, indicating the system is still moving towards equilibrium.
Option 4 is correct. This point on the graph shows that the concentrations of reactants and products have become constant, indicating that the system has reached equilibrium.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option 4. At equilibrium, the concentrations of reactants and products do not change over time, meaning they are constant. This is represented in the graph at point 4. The other options (1, 2, and 3) show points where the concentrations are still changing, indicating that equilibrium has not been reached.

questions.text: When NaNO3 is heated in a closed vessel, oxygen is liberated and NaNO2 is left behind, at equilibrium:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: Addition of NaNO2 favors forward reaction
options.explanation: Solids do not affect the position of equilibrium, as their concentration is constant. Therefore, adding NaNO2 does not favor the forward reaction.

options.text: Addition of NaNO3 favors forward reaction
options.explanation: Since NaNO3 is a solid, its addition does not affect the equilibrium position. Therefore, this option is incorrect.
The reaction is endothermic, meaning it absorbs heat. Increasing the temperature shifts the equilibrium towards the products to consume the added heat, thus favoring the forward reaction.

options.text: Both addition of NaNO3 and decreasing temperature favors forward reaction
options.explanation: Adding NaNO3 does not affect the equilibrium because it is a solid, and decreasing temperature would actually favor the reverse reaction in an endothermic process. Therefore, this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: In this endothermic reaction, increasing the temperature provides the necessary energy to drive the reaction towards the formation of products, thus favoring the forward reaction. Solids like NaNO3 and NaNO2 do not affect the equilibrium position as their concentrations remain constant, hence options involving the addition of these compounds are incorrect. Decreasing the temperature would favor the reverse reaction, as it would reduce the energy available for the endothermic forward reaction.
